summary: Gruber dissects The New Yorker's 16,000-word investigation into Sam Altman's trustworthiness, highlighting damning assessments from former colleagues including Aaron Swartz calling Altman a 'sociopath,' Microsoft executives comparing him to Bernie Madoff, and Paul Graham's conspicuous refusal to vouch for Altman's integrity. He examines the palace intrigue around Fidji Simo's sudden medical leave, speculating it may be a cover for Altman pushing her out after she angled to replace him. Gruber draws a pointed comparison between OpenAI and Enron — both companies with real technology but potentially fraudulent financial narratives. The piece concludes that while no smoking gun proves Altman dishonest, the pattern of distrust from those closest to him is damning, and the stakes of AI development make leadership integrity non-negotiable.
key_points: Aaron Swartz warned friends before his death that Altman is a 'sociopath' who 'can never be trusted' — new reporting that Gruber finds highly credible given Swartz's reputation for honesty
Paul Graham's public defense of Altman conspicuously avoids actually vouching for his honesty or integrity, only disputing the specific claim that Altman was 'fired' from Y Combinator
Microsoft executives describe Altman as someone who has 'misrepresented, distorted, renegotiated, reneged on agreements,' with one comparing him to Madoff or Bankman-Fried
Fidji Simo's sudden medical leave may be cover for Altman squeezing her out, and her hiring raises questions about Altman's judgment given Instacart's FTC settlement for deceptive practices
Gruber argues Enron is the better comparison to OpenAI than FTX — real technology underneath, but a financial story that appears 'alarmingly circular'
money_quotes: A recurring theme in the piece is that colleagues who've worked with Altman the closest trust him the least.
I think it's unambiguous that he's not a man of great integrity.
The most successful scams — the ones that last longest and grow largest — are ones with an actual product at the heart.
OpenAI's technology is undeniably real and blazing the frontier of AI. It's the financial story Altman has structured that seems alarmingly circular.
'We didn't remove Sam Altman' and 'We didn't want him to leave' are not the same things as saying 'I think Sam Altman is honest and trustworthy.'
If Paul Graham were to say such things, clearly and unambiguously, those remarks would carry tremendous weight. But — rather conspicuously to my eyes — he's not saying such things.
Simo changing her title to 'CEO of AGI deployment' is akin to changing her title to 'CEO of ghost busting' in terms of its literal practical responsibility.

It raises serious questions why — if Altman is a man of integrity who believes that OpenAI is a company whose nature demands leaders of especially high integrity — he would hire the Instacart CEO who spearheaded bait-and-switch consumer scams.
key_insight: The people who have worked most closely with Sam Altman consistently refuse to vouch for his integrity, and that pattern of distrust — from Swartz to Graham to Microsoft executives — matters enormously given OpenAI's outsized influence over the future of AI.
